_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
specialty: Urology, Pediatric Surgery, Emergency Room Packages
clinical_indications: Hematuria defined as the presence of 5 or more red blood cells (RBCs) per high-power field in 3 of 3 consecutive centrifuged specimens obtained at least 1 week apart.
Microscopic hematuria is a commonly seen condition in the emergency department.
Macroscopic hematuria has a high diagnostic yield for urological malignancy.
Associated symptoms present (e.g., inability to pass urine, evidence of clots, fever, urgency, urinary incontinence) which are suggestive of UTI or other underlying cause.

Goals of treatment in the Emergency Department (ED) include: resuscitation as appropriate; ensuring urine can drain freely with or without catheter insertion; safe discharge from ED where appropriate; prompt follow-up and further investigation.

clinical_thresholds.note: in 3 of 3 consecutive centrifuged specimens obtained at least 1 week apart

preauth.label: Clinical notes including evaluation findings and planned line of treatment

ppd_preauth.q: Was the Clinical notes including evaluation findings and planned line of treatment submitted?
Was the report related to urine (routine/ culture) submitted?
Was the USG abdomen report submitted?
Was the clinical notes and USG abdomen suggestive of any underline causes for Hematuria?

cpd_claim.q: Was the detailed indoor case papers (ICPs) with the treatment provided submitted?
Are the Detailed procedure notes submitted?
Was the detailed discharge summary submitted?

common_queries: Clinical notes and/or USG abdomen not suggestive of any underlying causes for Hematuria, thereby not justifying the emergency management.
Hematuria not defined by presence of 5 or more red blood cells per high-power field in 3 of 3 consecutive centrifuged specimens obtained at least 1 week apart.

Absence of associated symptoms (e.g., inability to pass urine, clots, fever, urgency, urinary incontinence) or predisposing factors (e.g., bleeding disorders, trauma) that would necessitate emergency management.
Missing or incomplete clinical notes, urine (routine/culture) reports, or USG abdomen report at pre-authorization stage.
Missing or incomplete detailed indoor case papers, detailed procedure notes, or detailed discharge summary at claim submission stage.

additional_information.min_doctor_qualification_desirable: MD in Emergency medicine; MCh / DNB (Urology/Pediatric surgery)
clinical_key_pointers: Proceed for Surgery only if diagnosis made is backed by clinical signs, symptoms, examination.
Hematuria can be either gross (i.e. overtly bloody, smoky, or tea-colored urine) or microscopic.
